item.content:encoded: Took enough time to harvest all this blood upvotes for the Daeva matriarchs upvote gods!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
World's latest reply to ever exist but to potentially clear things up for other people:

The posterior portion has a circular Thaumic Glyph Pattern (TGP) with a radius of 26cm, which prevents fire and erosion damage.

Magical protections were put in place for a lot of that stuff. Also, as far as headcanon goes, the Daevites were never technologically advanced. They certainly got better weaponry later on (part of why 3140s fell out of favor over time) but were never on the same level as say the Xia Dynasty. The common use of 3140s was because the Daevites could both grow stuff easily and use the occult easily. Proficient in magic but not tech.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But why would they make an almighty, programmable (I think?), deadly siege weapon… out of a tree, exactly? Wood burns. It can be hacked away, melted by acid, blown up. Why not steel, the Daevites seem advanced enough?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My headcanon of the Daevite is a Aztec-like civilisation living on black magic and was ruled by shark-toothed women who happen to love eating or castrating male salves. (Poor Nadox)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, I really enjoy the Sarkic-Daeva canon for the meritorious work it put on the history research. Comparing the two, I think that's what this entry is lacking in. That being said, I do agree the Sarkic canon is running in circle without real plot progression. As for this entry, despite the shortcoming, I like how it depict the evolution of technology in the hand of Daevas. Nice work. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With regards to how the Foundation is able to know so much about ancient languages (and speak some Daevic), I imagine that with anomalies like SCP-1726 around they have a ton of material to use for translation purposes (along with the possibility of anomalous means for translation, which is something I have semi-headcanoned). For the anomaly itself being able to survive for such a long time, "The posterior portion has a circular Thaumic Glyph Pattern (TGP) with a radius of 26cm, which prevents fire and erosion damage." I was hoping this would imply that this had kept SCP-3140-1 in a functional state despite how long it had been buried for.

I do understand why not explicitly stating this stuff would break suspension of disbelief, though. Ultimately the Foundation knowing all of this stuff was just a way to do worldbuilding, and I recognize that it can be seen as a flaw.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Note that the reason the I placed the time frame far back was to suggest the presence of far past civilizations (described to an extent in the documents (Erliontipa and Olute)), which were probably anomalous in some ways.

I see why you went there, but this is also why it didn't work for me.

+ show block
– hide block

There's a few angles I have seen with people talking about precursor civilizations, or secret civilizations with no history. Some of them don't put much work into integrating them with history, but those are stories about why the civilization is gone. We erased SCP-1000 from memory. The Xia dynasty erased themselves from history. Homo sapiens idaltu erased Homo sapiens descensus from history because of stuff like SCP-1427. We don't have even have much evidence for the Daeva, but we get more and more of it every time someone messes with the book.

On the other end, Mekhanite and Sarkic skips tend to make things being recovered at least somewhat plausible and integrate actual history in really legitimately interesting ways, such as SCP-2309 and SCP-2406. Hell, take the Daeva in SCP-392.

Here, however, it feels like you were so excited to dig into the deep past that you haven't done any of the work of making it seem lost to begin with. Ignoring how whole civilizations disappeared and focusing on this object alone:
- You have the Foundation able to use spoken Daevic1 commands, no explanation, despite the language being dead for millennia and only known in writing through SCP-2140 and maybe SCP-1726.
- The giant organic object (but only one of them) has survived relatively unharmed and functional for ten millennia. Again, no explanation.
- The only nod you have to the ancient documents being ancient are us not knowing the units of measure, despite us being able to pull out perfect grammar and words like "aether-benders" and "cannons." (Even the excerpts in 1726 are much shorter.)

The world-building in the translations has some neat imagery, but there's nothing real or tangible for me to peg it to, or to cause me to suspend my disbelief. The last translated log was the most grounded and interesting.

-1

Footnotes
1. And not even a specific dialect or era. See Ancient Greek's evolution for a comparison, and we don't even fully know how that was pronounced.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a fantastic entry that really helps expand on the Daevites in a meaningful way. It feels like the consequences of messing with their records (the bit about their history changing, even as "real" history continues to exclude them) are beginning to show the Foundation why they need to stop reading that book, damn it. It also helped push an idea that was, to me, interesting but overshadowed to the point that I can't wait to see more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Note that the reason the I placed the time frame far back was to suggest the presence of far past civilizations (described to an extent in the documents (Erliontipa and Olute)), which were probably anomalous in some ways. I also wanted to keep some consistency with SCP-1726, which describes the Daevite Empire collapsing prior to 9000 BCE.

Why there's little trace of these things is up to headcanon.

Glad you enjoyed reading this!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great stuff! The worldbuilding you've done is amazing, and I'm glad to see the Daevites get more articles. I really like the Ortothan material too.

The only thing that bugged me with this was the the Century Conquest happened earlier than 10.000 BCE, especially since they apparently fought "several Asian civilizations". Considering that most civilizations didn't arise until after 10.000 BCE, as that's roughly the time the Agricultural Revolution happened, it just seems strange that there wouldn't be any geological indications of agriculture or large scale horticulture if the Daevites had such a large empire. Besides, what civilizations would they have been fighting against? Besides the Ortothans, that is.

I'd suggest putting the years a bit later. You could probably put it between 8.000 and 3.000 BCE and still have it elude recorded history, considering that the oldest deciphered writing system dates from around -3.000. A plus is you could also use the timeframe to add a bit of immersion, maybe by saying that the Daevite-Ortothan conflict caused the collapse of the Indus Valley Civilization (the cause of which is still contested today).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is all great, but I think my favourite thing about it is that it builds on the concept that there are just huge portions of ancient history that have been lost or suppressed from the public eye.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has some really great worldbuilding, and really fills in the idea of a "second world" existing inside the Foundation Universe, where Weird Shit Happens™.

I really enjoyed this, and I hope to see more of things like this. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…Daevite soldiers killing and eating people, soldiers with unidentified megafauna and entities resembling SCP-3140 besieging castles and cities, and slaves being given to the Daeva matriarchs.

Yeah, if you had them living next door they would probably be annoyingly noisy around the clock.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm getting the impression that the Daevas would not have been agreeable to have as neighbors.

This is enjoyable world-building. Arboreal golems are a good addition to the Foundationverse. Using one as farm labor is a GREAT addition.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only Djoric Daevite thing I've read is SCP-1726 so I don't entirely know how he imagines them, but that's pretty similar to my general headcanon on them (but with more botany).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Djoric details his headcanon in a lot of his stories; my read on it based on that is, basically modern society but with magic instead of electricity. Also constant rivers of blood to appease gods and goddesses with a penchant for going Old Testament at the slightest provocation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seriously, someone should make an article compiling Daevite culture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You had me at tree-mecha.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At this rate, Daevite culture is going to be all about plants and weird symbols!

Yes but those plants have roots embedded in the skeletons of slaves and those symbols are all 9,000 Daevite words for 'blood harvest.'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a sucker for historical worldbuilding, and this really scratched that itch. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: HARDCORE plants and weird symbols. Much cooler than normal boring plants and weird symbols.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love that this has pictures now and I think they really add to the article. I like that this breaks out of the rut a lot of recent Daeva and similar articles have fallen into. This is memorable on its own terms while also building up the Daeva mythology. Enthusiastic +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Daevite botany is hardcore.

At this rate, Daevite culture is going to be all about plants and weird symbols!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Accomplishes the world building you want it to, yet still has enough 'anomaly' to avoid just being a plain lore dump. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Daevite botany is hardcore.

(and worldbuilding is fun)

Thanks to weryllium, Dr0Shadow, Roget, DrBleep, salvagebar, ARD, spectraldragon, Balthazaar, and Varaxous for crit! (Let me know if I missed your name)

What I listened to (the most) while writing: Coda - BLOODY STREAM, The DU - Crazy Noisy Bizarre Town, Portal Soundtrack.

Daevic symbols drawn by myself in Gimp.

Dig site photo: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Archaeological_dig,_Whithorn_Priory_-_geograph.org.uk_-_262987.jpg (taken by Ann Cook)

Greenhouse photo: https://www.flickr.com/photos/usdagov/24931867396 (taken by the U.S. Department of Agriculture)
